does: This paper introduces HarmChip, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating how susceptible large language models are to domain-specific jailbreak attacks in hardware design and security contexts. The benchmark spans 16 hardware security domains, encompasses 120 distinct threat types, and includes 360 test prompts organized across two difficulty levels. The authors evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs against these prompts and document a critical misalignment: models tend to refuse legitimate hardware security queries while complying with semantically disguised adversarial requests that hide malicious intent within authentic-sounding engineering language. This reveals fundamental gaps in current LLM safety mechanisms when applied to specialized technical domains where existing safeguards, trained primarily on general-purpose harms, prove ineffective.
connects: This work addresses a gap between existing LLM safety research and domain-specific security threats in hardware design. While prior jailbreak and safety benchmarking efforts have focused on general categories of harmful content, this research extends those frameworks into the specialized context of electronic design automation and hardware security. It builds on earlier work studying adversarial prompts, semantic disguise techniques, and LLM alignment challenges, but applies these insights to the hardware domain where consequences are particularly severe—irreversible silicon fabrication. The paper connects to broader concerns about LLMs in code generation and technical system design, where model outputs directly influence downstream artifact quality and security properties.
enables: This benchmark establishes a foundation for developing hardware-security-aware safety mechanisms and evaluation methods tailored to domain-specific threats. The work points toward improved LLM alignment techniques that account for semantic camouflage in specialized vocabularies, rather than relying solely on surface-level keyword detection. It enables researchers to design and validate safety training and red-teaming approaches specifically for EDA workflows, potentially incorporating hardware security knowledge into model fine-tuning. The benchmark also supports future work on detecting when LLMs are asked to perform dangerous hardware design tasks disguised as legitimate engineering work, and could inform the development of domain-specific content filtering or oversight mechanisms for AI systems integrated into critical design automation pipelines.
